content:
This is a decent clash.  
Interesting in reverse mode,  since one must come to the idea to use the ascii value (and will quickly find out that it's pi related just noting that it's a little more than times 3 and the division will immediately enlighten)
Also decent in shortest mode, even if it seems trivial in fastest mode.  
There is one issue in the constraint:  you give a competitive advantage to python programmers, since pi is defined differently in other languages (e.g. std::numbers::pi in C++,  M_PI with the right include in C, Double.pi in Swift,  Math.pi  in Java or JavaScript, not to speak of the acos(-1) alternative.    

==> Suggestion

"The π constant should be defined in your language library.  If not you may consider the alternative acos(-1) or the approximation 3,14159"

❌ Rejected the contribution.
Comment: prescence of a magic number
I agree with Diabetos. Using a magic number would make this near impossible in my opinion

❌ Rejected the contribution.
Comment: First of all, as 5DN1L pointed, you could have not written any statement, but that's not the reason of my refusal (EDIT: that's not really what he said my bad, but still).
I really think a magic number in a reverse clash is a bad thing, I'd never call a library just for Pi, I am not a professionnal, so maybe it is I who built bad habits, but I don't think you should HAVE to call one for a clash, you should be able to do it yourself, and who knows Pi further than what even you wrote (to pass Test 2 you need 3.141593)

Goal
* Please add at the beginning:
<<This clash is in Reverse mode only.>>

Thanks.
